title: Improving Language Model Pretraining with Text Structure Information

structured.summary: This paper investigates the impact of text complexity on language model pretraining by comparing models trained on human-written educational text versus LLM-simplified versions preserving core semantics. Experiments with models from 28M to 500M parameters show that simplified text yields comparable downstream fine-tuning performance, benefits smaller models in language modeling perplexity, and differentially affects zero-shot generalization across linguistic and knowledge-intensive tasks.

strengths.point: Controlled comparison using parallel corpora differing only in surface-level complexity
strengths.evidence: The study uses a subset of FineWeb-Edu corpus and its LLaMA 3.1 8B simplified counterpart, verified by semantic similarity (~79%) and multiple readability and lexical metrics.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation across model sizes and multiple downstream tasks
strengths.evidence: Models ranging from 28M to 500M parameters were trained and evaluated on GLUE/SuperGLUE tasks with both full fine-tuning and linear probing, as well as zero-shot evaluation on diverse linguistic and commonsense benchmarks.

strengths.point: Insightful findings on interaction between model capacity and text complexity
strengths.evidence: Smaller models show less perplexity degradation on simplified text, indicating simpler text is easier to model for limited-capacity models, while larger models perform similarly regardless of text complexity.

strengths.point: Identification of nuanced effects of text complexity on zero-shot generalization
strengths.evidence: Simplified text models slightly outperform on linguistic knowledge tasks, whereas human-written text models excel on entity/world knowledge and some commonsense tasks.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient engagement with prior work on curriculum learning and data filtering
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not explicitly compare or discuss seminal works like Bengio et al. (2009) or Raffel et al. (2020), weakening claims of novelty.

weaknesses.point: Simplification methodology lacks quantitative hallucination or semantic drift analysis
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation or error analysis quantifies how often LLaMA-based simplification alters factual content or introduces inconsistencies.

weaknesses.point: Limited dataset size and domain restrict generalizability
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments use ~2B tokens from educational text only, contrasting with typical large-scale, multi-domain pretraining corpora.

weaknesses.point: Training limited to one epoch and relatively small models
weaknesses.evidence: Models up to 500M parameters trained for a single epoch, possibly obscuring effects in larger or fully trained models.

weaknesses.point: No ablation isolating lexical, syntactic, or structural simplification effects
weaknesses.evidence: While simplification is verified via multiple metrics, the paper does not disentangle which aspects most impact modeling or downstream tasks.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation excludes generative or open-ended tasks
weaknesses.evidence: Focus is on classification and multiple-choice benchmarks, limiting understanding of text complexity effects on generation or reasoning.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hindered by missing details on prompts, hyperparameters, and random seeds
weaknesses.evidence: Exact LLaMA 3.1 prompt templates, training schedules, batch sizes, and seeds are not specified.

weaknesses.point: No linguistic error analysis explaining low performance on coreference/discourse tasks
weaknesses.evidence: Tasks like WSC and MultiRC show low scores without analysis of underlying linguistic phenomena affected by simplification.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of potential biases introduced by simplification
weaknesses.evidence: Simplification may remove rare vocabulary or complex structures, but this is not analyzed or addressed.

weaknesses.point: Lack of statistical significance testing and effect size reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Performance differences are described qualitatively without confidence intervals or hypothesis tests.

questions.question: How would the findings generalize to larger, more diverse pretraining corpora and longer training schedules?
questions.motivation: Understanding scalability is critical for applying insights to state-of-the-art large language models trained on heterogeneous data.

questions.question: What is the impact of text complexity on generative and open-ended language tasks?
questions.motivation: Since generation and reasoning may rely differently on text complexity, evaluating these tasks would clarify the broader applicability of simplified pretraining data.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient engagement with prior work on curriculum learning and data filtering. The paper does not explicitly compare or discuss seminal works like Bengio et al. (2009) or Raffel et al. (2020), weakening claims of novelty
critique_points.point_002: Simplification methodology lacks quantitative hallucination or semantic drift analysis. No ablation or error analysis quantifies how often LLaMA-based simplification alters factual content or introduces inconsistencies
critique_points.point_003: Limited dataset size and domain restrict generalizability. Experiments use ~2B tokens from educational text only, contrasting with typical large-scale, multi-domain pretraining corpora
critique_points.point_004: Training limited to one epoch and relatively small models. Models up to 500M parameters trained for a single epoch, possibly obscuring effects in larger or fully trained models
critique_points.point_005: No ablation isolating lexical, syntactic, or structural simplification effects. While simplification is verified via multiple metrics, the paper does not disentangle which aspects most impact modeling or downstream tasks
critique_points.point_006: Evaluation excludes generative or open-ended tasks. Focus is on classification and multiple-choice benchmarks, limiting understanding of text complexity effects on generation or reasoning
critique_points.point_007: Reproducibility hindered by missing details on prompts, hyperparameters, and random seeds. Exact LLaMA 3.1 prompt templates, training schedules, batch sizes, and seeds are not specified
critique_points.point_008: No linguistic error analysis explaining low performance on coreference/discourse tasks. Tasks like WSC and MultiRC show low scores without analysis of underlying linguistic phenomena affected by simplification
critique_points.point_009: No discussion of potential biases introduced by simplification. Simplification may remove rare vocabulary or complex structures, but this is not analyzed or addressed
critique_points.point_010: Lack of statistical significance testing and effect size reporting. Performance differences are described qualitatively without confidence intervals or hypothesis tests
